citation: Hu FB, Stampfer MJ, Rimm EB, et al. "A Prospective Study of Egg Consumption and Risk of Cardiovascular Disease in Men and Women." JAMA, 1999.
claim_summary: Large U.S. prospective cohort study (drawing on the Nurses' Health Study and Health Professionals Follow-up Study) examining the association between frequency of egg consumption and risk of coronary heart disease and stroke. Landmark reference in the egg/dietary-cholesterol literature.

citation: U.S. Department of Agriculture / U.S. Department of Health and Human Services. Dietary Guidelines for Americans (multiple editions).
claim_summary: Federal dietary guidance document, issued in successive 5-year editions, addressing recommended limits on dietary cholesterol intake. The specific numeric guidance on dietary cholesterol is known to have changed across editions (earlier editions carried an explicit daily cap; a later edition revised this treatment). Exact edition, wording, and year of any change must be verified before citing — do not assume which edition said what.

citation: Keys A, et al. The Seven Countries Study (multi-country epidemiological research program, mid-20th century).
claim_summary: Foundational mid-20th-century multi-country epidemiological research program correlating dietary fat patterns with serum cholesterol levels and coronary heart disease rates. Underlies the broader 'diet-heart hypothesis' literature that later, more specific egg/dietary-cholesterol studies respond to and revise.

citation: Rong Y, et al. "Egg consumption and risk of coronary heart disease and stroke: dose-response meta-analysis of prospective cohort studies." BMJ, 2013.
claim_summary: Dose-response meta-analysis of prospective cohort studies on egg consumption and cardiovascular outcomes. Widely cited for reporting no overall association in the general population while noting a differing picture among people with diabetes. Exact effect sizes and subgroup details must be verified before citing. Serves here as a second study yielding multiple findings (a study-grouping demonstration for friction F2).
